pattern: Because the grid holds distinct values in a known range, index each value to its (row, col) once at construction, then answer a query by summing the in-bounds cells offset by the four cardinal or four diagonal directions.
hints: Each value appears exactly once, so you never need to scan the whole grid per query if you remember where each value lives.
Build a lookup from value to its row and column while reading the grid in the constructor.
Adjacent uses the four offsets (+/-1 in one axis); diagonal uses the four offsets (+/-1 in both axes); skip any neighbor that falls outside the grid bounds.

edge_inputs.description: statement example one: adjacent and diagonal queries on a 3x3 grid including a center cell and a corner
statement example two: adjacent and diagonal queries on a 4x4 grid
top-left corner has only one adjacent and one diagonal neighbor
top-right corner: adjacent neighbors only to left and below, diagonal only bottom-left
an edge (non-corner) cell on the top row has three adjacent and two diagonal neighbors, not four
diagonalSum must ignore the cardinal neighbors entirely for an interior cell
repeated queries on the same value return the same sum, exercising the lookup cache
bottom-right corner of a 3x3 grid: single adjacent-up/left and single diagonal neighbor

stress.description: random n x n grid (3<=n<=10) of a shuffled permutation of [0, n*n-1], with interleaved adjacent/diagonal queries over every value
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(3, 10)
    perm = list(range(n * n))
    rng.shuffle(perm)
    grid = [perm[r * n:(r + 1) * n] for r in range(n)]
    ops = ['NeighborSum']
    args = [[grid]]
    for _ in range(max(1, size)):
        v = rng.randint(0, n * n - 1)
        if rng.random() < 0.5:
            ops.append('adjacentSum')
        else:
            ops.append('diagonalSum')
        args.append([v])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: distinct values guaranteed by permutation; query space covers corners, edges, and interior cells
